query: does giant cell arteritis present with palpable purpura

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should suspect GCA in any patient older than 50 who presents with a new headache or change in the typical features of their preexisting headache. Other scenarios that warrant suspicion in such patients are jaw claudication, unexplained fever or other constitutional symptoms, abrupt visual disturbance or loss, and signs of vascular abnormalities. [12] Tongue pain is rare but, if present, significantly increases the likelihood of GCA. [9] Vascular abnormalities may manifest as limb claudication, asymmetric blood pressures, abnormal radial pulses, vascular bruits, and temporal artery abnormalities such as tenderness to palpation, decreased pulse amplitude, and the presence of nodules.

retrieved_docs.title: Takayasu Arteritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Aortic coarctation Atherosclerosis Behcet disease Giant cell  arteritis Ig-G4 related disease Kawasaki disease Rheumatoid arthritis Sarcoidosis

retrieved_docs.title: Granulomatosis With Polyangiitis -- History and Physical -- Generalized Systemic Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Skin: Dermatologic involvement is reported in 50% to 60% of patients with GPA, with purpura commonly involving the lower extremities. Other common manifestations, including cutaneous nodules, particularly involving the olecranon regions, are common and can be mistaken for rheumatoid arthritis. Less commonly, ulcers, papules, vesicles, and subcutaneous nodules (granulomas) may occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Cogan Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Takayasu arteritis [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Cogan Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Polyarteritis nodosa

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Etiology -- Noninfectious Inflammatory Causes of FUO
retrieved_docs.content: Giant cell (temporal) arteritis Adult Still disease (juvenile rheumatoid arthritis) Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) Periarteritis nodosa/microscopic polyangiitis (PAN/MPA) Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) Gout Pseudogout Behçet disease Sarcoidosis Felty syndrome Takayasu arteritis Kikuchi disease Periodic fever adenitis pharyngitis aphthous ulcer (PFAPA) syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A current or previous diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica should also raise clinical suspicion for GCA. Any patient with a diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica should undergo evaluation for GCA. Clinicians should inquire about headaches, jaw or arm claudication, visual symptoms, and unusual facial, throat, or tongue pain. At a minimum, patients with polymyalgia rheumatica should undergo carotid and subclavian artery auscultation for bruits and bilateral blood pressure measurements.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein Purpura) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of IgA vasculitis is made based on the presence of petechiae (without thrombocytopenia) or palpable purpura that predominantly affects the lower limbs plus at least one of the following four characteristics: [1] Abdominal pain Arthralgia or arthritis Renal involvement (proteinuria, red blood cell casts, or hematuria) Proliferative glomerulonephritis or leukocytoclastic vasculitis with predominant deposition of IgA on histology

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein Purpura) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The classic presentation of IgA vasculitis includes palpable purpura, gastrointestinal complaints, arthralgias, and renal involvement. Patients may present with any of the following signs or symptoms: Rash Fatigue Headache Fever Joint pain Subcutaneous edema Diarrhea Hematemesis Abdominal pain Vomiting Rectal bleeding Scrotal edema

retrieved_docs.title: 38-Year-Old Male Presents With Lower Extremity Edema -- Differential Diagnosis -- Vasculitis
retrieved_docs.content: Vasculitis including granulomatosis with polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa, Churg-Strauss Syndrome, IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinemia, immune complex-associated vasculitis (infectious, SLE, or drug hypersensitivity), giant cell arteritis, leukocytoclastic vasculitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Microscopic Polyangiitis -- History and Physical -- Skin Manifestations
retrieved_docs.content: Skin lesions are observed in 30% to 60% of patients with MPA, and these lesions are the initial presenting feature in 15% to 30% of cases. [22] [23] These skin manifestations include palpable purpura, livedo reticularis, urticaria, nodules, and ulcers with necrosis. Additionally, skin manifestations have been associated with joint pain in patients with MPA (see Image. Typical Skin Manifestations Observed in Microscopic Polyangiitis). [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Leukocytoclastic Vasculitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vasculitis refers to inflammation of the blood vessels leading to tissue destruction with or without organ damage. Vasculitis is classified as small vessel, medium vessel or large vessel vasculitis [1] and maybe either idiopathic or associated with an underlying pathology/disease.  Small vessel vasculitis can be seen secondary to systemic vasculitides such as Anti-neutrophil Cytoplasmic Antibody (ANCA) associated vasculitis (Microscopic polyangiitis, Granulomatosis with polyangiitis or Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis), Behçet’s disease, and Cogan’s syndrome. Immune complex-mediated small vessel vasculitis can be seen in rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjogren syndrome, Henoch-Schönlein purpura, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, Hypocomplementemic urticarial vasculitis, Erythema elevatum diutinum, and cutaneous leukocytoclastic angiitis, formerly known as hypersensitivity vasculitis.  Other causes of small vessel vasculitis or leukocytoclastic vasculitis include drug-induced vasculitis, paraneoplastic vasculitis, and infection associated vasculitis (hepatitis B, hepatitis C, syphilis).

retrieved_docs.title: Vasculitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Specific clinical abnormalities that, when present alone or in combination, should suggest a diagnosis of vasculitis. These include palpable purpura, pulmonary infiltrates and microscopic hematuria, chronic inflammatory sinusitis, mononeuritis multiplex, unexplained ischemic events, and glomerulonephritis with evidence of multisystem disease. In this activity, the discussion will focus on GCA and ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV).

retrieved_docs.title: Purpura Fulminans -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: If suspecting purpura fulminans, assume it is the acute infectious form until proven otherwise. Diagnosis may be difficult because pathognomonic features can be lacking, so a high level of suspicion needs to be maintained. Pain out of proportion to physical findings is always concerning and may be the key to diagnosing necrotizing fasciitis. Consulting surgical services should be involved early in the process.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein Purpura) -- History and Physical -- Joints
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 15% of patients with IgA vasculitis present with arthritis as the initial symptom, and overall arthralgia or arthritis occurs in 75% of children with the disorder. [9] Patients often present with painful, swollen joints that most commonly involve the knees, ankles, hands, and feet. The arthralgias are typically transient and non-destructive.

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis) -- History and Physical -- Cranial Symptoms and Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Large vessel involvement: Large vessel involvement in GCA generally refers to the aorta and its major proximal branches, especially in the upper extremities. Initial symptoms may be upper extremity claudication, back or chest pain due to aortitis, or aortic dissection. [9] Auscultation should be regularly performed. Clinicians may appreciate bruits on auscultation of the carotid or supraclavicular areas, over the axillary, brachial, or femoral arteries, and the abdominal aorta. Additional physical examination findings include a lack of upper extremity pulses, asymmetric pulses and blood pressure readings in the upper extremities, and Raynaud phenomenon with or without digital ischemia. Aortic aneurysms, with thoracic being more common than abdominal ones, may also form. [16] A heart murmur associated with aortic regurgitation may signify an ascending aortic aneurysm.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1317
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic cardiac involvement that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when evaluating patients with rheumatic heart disease, as it helps differentiate these acute forms from more chronic rheumatic cardiac conditions and guides timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are all characterized by a clinical course of sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of sudden onset and/or short duration also defines the clinical course of الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic inflammation) and of احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute anterior‑wall myocardial injury). Recognizing this rapid and brief presentation is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of these rheumatic cardiac conditions and related acute cardiac events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are all described as having a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. The same rapid‑onset, brief‑duration pattern is also attributed to التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis) and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (an unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease). Collectively, these forms of acute rheumatic heart disease share the characteristic of a swift and often brief clinical presentation, which is important for timely diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2616
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, acute rheumatic myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد) and acute rheumatic pericarditis (الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ) are all characterized by a clinical course that is sudden in onset and/or of short duration. Recognizing this rapid presentation across the various forms of acute rheumatic heart disease helps clinicians diagnose and manage these conditions promptly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية) is clinically associated with other circulatory disease, and this category of circulatory disease is classified as a subtype of connective tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة). This relationship underscores that cardiovascular manifestations observed in lupus are part of the broader spectrum of connective tissue diseases, highlighting the importance of integrated cardiovascular evaluation and management in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis and acute rheumatic valvulitis are both rheumatic heart conditions whose clinical course is characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. The same rapid and brief clinical pattern is also described for the Arabic‑named entities التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease). Together, these English and Arabic terms indicate that acute rheumatic involvement of the heart—whether affecting the whole heart (pancarditis), the valves, the myocardium, the pericardium, or an unspecified cardiac component—typically presents with a rapid onset and a relatively short disease course, a feature that is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2389
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic valvulitis is characterized by a clinical course of sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of sudden onset and/or short duration is also the defining clinical course of several related rheumatic heart conditions, including التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُور الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease), and احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute inflammation of the anterior heart wall). Recognizing this shared rapid and brief clinical presentation aids clinicians in promptly identifying and managing the spectrum of acute rheumatic cardiac disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2867
retrieved_docs.content: Other diseases of veins and lymphatics have a broader relationship with an unspecified disorder of the circulatory system, indicating that these venous and lymphatic conditions are encompassed within the larger category of undefined circulatory system disturbances. Recognizing this relationship can aid clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of patients presenting with ambiguous circulatory symptoms.
